topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammer against the wooden posts was a relentless, percussive assault on my fragile peace. Every morning, the sight of Mr. Henderson’s construction crew setting up their equipment sent a cold knot tightening in my chest. I kept staring out my window, my knuckles white where I gripped the mug of tea, trying to will the noise away. It wasn't just the noise; it was the sheer, unavoidable imposition of his plans, the certainty of the barrier rising between us. I paced the length of my living room three times before finally sinking onto the sofa, my gaze fixed on the sliver of sky visible between the scaffolding. I felt a deep, churning restlessness, a physical need to bolt, to disappear.

[story 2]

"Just a few more days, Martha," Mr. Henderson called out, his voice booming across the lawn. The sound vibrated through the glass of my kitchen window, making the silverware rattle. I couldn't meet his eyes; instead, I meticulously wiped down the already spotless counter, my movements jerky and too fast. My breath hitched in my throat, shallow and quick. I kept running my hands over the worn fabric of my cardigan, seeking some anchor in the tangible. The thought of the fence—that stark, unforgiving line—made my stomach clench with a tight, acidic sensation. I found myself chewing the inside of my cheek, a small, involuntary motion, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I spent the entire afternoon tracing the outline of the proposed fence on my notepad, the pencil tip digging into the paper until the wood threatened to tear. Each line he drew seemed to solidify the encroaching boundary, squeezing the air from my lungs with every stroke. When he stopped by the gate to check the alignment, his casual demeanor felt like a deliberate provocation. I stood rigid by the mailbox, my hands clenched so tightly my fingernails bit into my palms. I could feel a tremor running through my entire frame, a deep, internal shaking that made my vision swim slightly. I wanted to scream, but the sound caught somewhere behind my teeth, leaving only a dry, rasping silence.
